content:
Asia's richest municipal body runs Mumbai on a budget of over ₹74,000 crore. Yet every monsoon, the city fills up with potholes. Why?

In our recent episode, Ashwini Bhide, IAS officer, BMC Commissioner and MD of Mumbai Metro Rail Corporation, gave an explanation for this. 

The potholes aren't a sign of a city that lacks money. They're a symptom of what's buried under the roads.

Mumbai has around 2,050 km of roads, and beneath almost all of them runs the city's entire nervous system, water lines, drainage, electricity, communication cables. 

As the population grows and new buildings come up, those utilities constantly need new connections. 

When a line needs a repair or a new building needs a hookup, the road above has to be dug up. 

That digging, repeated across a dense and constantly expanding city, is what keeps breaking the surface open.

She explained that the pothole is often just the visible scar of work happening underneath.

The budget to fill potholes across the city's regular roads is around ₹40 to ₹50 crore, with another ₹60 crore for the Eastern and Western highways, roughly ₹100 crore in total. 

Against a budget this size, that sounds easy. But money was never the bottleneck. 

The challenge is that in a city this dense, you cannot simply shut down roads for months to rebuild them properly from the ground up. 

Life doesn't pause. Traffic doesn't stop. So the work happens in patches, under pressure, in the small windows the city allows.

Mumbai's problems stem from the complexity of maintaining a megacity where 20 million people live on top of infrastructure that needs constant repair, without ever being able to close the city down to do so.

Full episode out now, watch here: https://lnkd.in/drpfJNCf

comment: The harder infrastructure question is whether cities can move from reactive repairs to coordinated utility mapping and shared corridors, so every new connection doesn't repeatedly reopen the same road.
